stories.title: Historic Cinerama Dome, Adjacent 14-Screen Complex Set for 2028 Reopening
stories.excerpt: Shuttered since the pandemic, the historic Cinerama Dome in Hollywood and surrounding 14-screen movie theater are set to reopen under the management of Alamo Drafthouse Cinema, a unit of Sony Pictures Entertainment. CBRE’s Nicole Mihalka, along with Carl Muhlstein of Muhlstein CRE, and Scott Burns and Shauna Mattis of JLL jointly represented the ownership of the Dome Entertainment Center, while JLL’s Ken Shishido represented Alamo Drafthouse. Opened in 1963, the Dome will undergo restorative renovations starting next month through early 2028. Its historic legacy and singular moviegoing experience will be honored and carefully preserved, with the iconic “Cinerama” name and exterior branding left intact. The adjacent complex, formerly run by ArcLight Cinemas, will be operated as an Alamo Drafthouse dine-in cinema. “This lease marks the beginning of an exciting new chapter for one of Hollywood’s most iconic and beloved properties,” said Mihalka. “The Cinerama Dome has been a cultural landmark for generations, and bringing Sony Entertainment and Alamo Drafthouse to the Dome Entertainment Center is a strong vote of confidence in the future of theatrical entertainment and the continued demand for shared moviegoing experiences.” The post Historic Cinerama Dome, Adjacent 14-Screen Complex Set for 2028 Reopening appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Cinerama Dome + 14-screen ArcLight complex reopening early 2028 under Sony/Alamo Drafthouse.
Historic Hollywood property shuttered since pandemic, undergoes restorative renovation starting next month.
Sony exec signals confidence in theatrical entertainment demand and shared moviegoing experiences.
stories.summary: Sony Pictures Entertainment and Alamo Drafthouse Cinema have secured a long-term lease to restore and operate the shuttered Cinerama Dome and adjacent 14-screen former ArcLight Cinemas in Hollywood. Renovations begin next month with reopening targeted for early 2028. Sony's Motion Picture Group CEO Tom Rothman framed the investment as a vote of confidence in theatrical moviegoing demand and Hollywood real estate.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily relevance. However, reopening of major entertainment anchors in Hollywood signals renewed faith in foot traffic and mixed-use vibrancy—supports demand for urban rental housing near cultural/entertainment districts in Los Angeles, though the Hollywood submarket is supply-constrained and value-add focused rather than development-stage.

stories.title: Roselle Park Apartments Trade After Brokers Resolve Permit Issue
stories.excerpt: Roselle Grande, a 119-unit garden-style apartment community located at 220-250 W. Sumner Ave. in Roselle Park, NJ, has changed hands for an undisclosed price, in a transaction arranged by SAGE Real Estate Advisors. The firm’s brokerage team of partners and co-founders Steve Tragash and Greg Pine, alongside principal Eli Herskowitz and associate Katie Migner, exclusively represented the seller, Roselle Grande LLC, and procured the unnamed buyer. During the seller’s deliverables phase, the transaction encountered an unresolved permit issue. Recognizing the importance of a coordinated effort, SAGE spearheaded a resolution for which Tragash and Migner spent nearly a month consulting with and engaging engineering companies to provide the town’s construction department with required documentation. “Today’s commercial real estate environment requires an engaged and knowledgeable advisor who is willing to step outside the traditional box,” said Tragash. Built in 1970 and situated on a parcel at the intersection of Roselle Park and Kenilworth, Roselle Grande consists of four buildings with a mix of studio, one-bedroom and two-bedroom layouts. The stabilized property features historically high occupancy rates, on-site parking and dedicated laundry facilities. The post Roselle Park Apartments Trade After Brokers Resolve Permit Issue appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Roselle Grande, 119-unit garden complex, sold in New Jersey after permit resolution.
Broker navigated month-long engineering/permitting coordination to close deal.
Stabilized property with high occupancy, built 1970, mix of studio/1BR/2BR units.
stories.summary: Roselle Grande, a 119-unit stabilized garden-style apartment community in Roselle Park, NJ, changed hands in a deal brokered by SAGE Real Estate Advisors. The transaction faced an unresolved permit issue that brokers resolved through month-long coordination with engineering firms and the town construction department. The 1970-built property features high occupancy, on-site parking, and dedicated laundry.
stories.impact: Minimal impact. This is a mature, stabilized, non-development transaction in suburban New Jersey. The permit-resolution narrative suggests regulatory friction in the Northeast, but no signal of new demand, supply disruption, or capital/cost shifts affecting development pipelines in Roselle Park or greater Newark metro.

stories.title: Jon Pezzoni Joins Hunneman’s Leasing & Advisory Team as SVP
stories.excerpt: Hunneman said Wednesday that Jon Pezzoni has joined the firm’s Leasing & Advisory team as an SVP and Principal. He brings more than a decade of experience across Greater Boston’s office, R&D and industrial markets. Pezzoni was most recently an SVP at Avison Young. Since 2014, Pezzoni has been advising tenants, landlords, and developers throughout the Metro West, 495 West & South submarkets, devising and executing strategies that best position assets to maximize their value. “Jon has built an outstanding reputation throughout the Metro West market by consistently delivering exceptional results for his clients,” said Steve Prozinski, CEO & COO of Hunneman. “His expertise across office, R&D, and industrial properties, combined with his collaborative approach and deep local relationships, make him an excellent addition to our Leasing & Advisory team. We’re excited to welcome Jon to Hunneman and look forward to the value he’ll bring to both our clients and our firm.” The post Jon Pezzoni Joins Hunneman’s Leasing & Advisory Team as SVP appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Jon Pezzoni joins Hunneman as SVP with 10+ years Boston office/R&D/industrial experience.
Focused on Metro West, 495 West & South submarkets since 2014.
Hire signals broker consolidation and talent migration in greater Boston market.
stories.summary: Jon Pezzoni, formerly SVP at Avison Young, joined Hunneman's Leasing & Advisory team as SVP and Principal. He brings over a decade advising tenants, landlords, and developers across Boston's office, R&D, and industrial markets, with deep expertise in Metro West, 495 West & South submarkets. Hunneman cited his relationships and strategic positioning of assets.
stories.impact: No material impact to multifamily development. Pezzoni's background is office, R&D, and industrial; no signal regarding residential rental demand, supply, capital, or construction costs in Boston metros.

stories.title: Marcus & Millichap Closes NorCal Seniors Housing Sale
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap closed the sale of Redwood Meadows, a 101-unit senior multifamily property located at 1475 Baechtel Rd. in Willits. The property sold for $10.8 million, or $106,931 per unit. “Senior housing communities with stable occupancy and long-term growth potential continue to attract investor interest across Northern California,” said Isaak Heitzeberg, managing director investments. “Redwood Meadows offered a combination of consistent cash flow, the potential to add up to 15 additional units with initial city approval and an assumable loan, making it an attractive long-term investment for a buyer expanding its regional portfolio.” Heitzeberg, of Marcus & Millichap’s Sacramento office, represented the seller, TCC Properties. Heitzeberg and Andres Guerra, investment specialist in Marcus & Millichap’s Sacramento office, also procured the buyer, Echelon Communities. Built in 1989, Redwood Meadows is a 101-unit active senior apartment community situated on a 7.18-acre parcel. The site also includes land with initial city approval for up to 15 additional units, offering future expansion potential. The post Marcus & Millichap Closes NorCal Seniors Housing Sale appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Marcus & Millichap closed sale of 101-unit senior housing (Redwood Meadows) in Willits, CA for $10.8M ($106.9k/unit).
Site has city approval for 15 additional units; assumable loan structure attracted buyer.
Buyer Echelon Communities expanding regional portfolio; stable occupancy and growth cited as draw.
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap brokered the sale of Redwood Meadows, a 101-unit senior multifamily property in Willits, Northern California, for $10.8 million ($106,931/unit). The 1989-built asset sits on 7.18 acres with city-approved expansion potential for 15 additional units and an assumable loan, attracting buyer Echelon Communities, which is growing its regional portfolio. Investor interest in senior housing with stable occupancy and expansion upside remains active.
stories.impact: Senior housing demand in NorCal remains solid, but limited relevance to mainstream multifamily development. The $106.9k/unit price in rural Willits reflects niche, stabilized senior-focused operations rather than market signals for market-rate apartment development or supply constraints in major metros.

stories.title: Alonzo Mourning, HTG net $55M financing package for affordable housing
stories.excerpt: NBA hall-of-famer Alonzo Mourning and Housing Trust Group scored a $55.4 million financing package for a Naranja affordable housing complex. The deal comes as the duo has racked up financing in recent years for below-market rate rentals in Miami-Dade County.  Mourning, through his AM Affordable Housing, and Coconut Grove-based Housing Trust Group plan the 115-unit Artisan Pointe project at 26115 South Dixie Highway in an unincorporated area of south Miami-Dade County. The four-story complex is under construction with completion expected in January 2028, according to the developers’ news release.  The financing package includes $31.5 million in low-income housing tax credit […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Alonzo Mourning & Housing Trust Group close $55.4M financing for 115-unit affordable project in Naranja, Miami-Dade.
$31.5M LIHTC equity, $16.9M permanent mortgage (Berkadia), $7M CRA/TIF funding; completion Jan 2028.
Rents: $766/mo (1BR @30% AMI) to $2,145/mo (2BR @70% AMI); market rents $1,350–$2,250.
stories.summary: Alonzo Mourning's AM Affordable Housing and Housing Trust Group secured $55.4 million in financing for Artisan Pointe, a 115-unit affordable complex under construction in Naranja (unincorporated south Miami-Dade). The deal includes $31.5M in LIHTC equity, a $16.9M permanent mortgage via Berkadia, and $7M in CRA/TIF funding. The four-story project targets households at 30%, 60%, and 70% AMI with completion expected January 2028.
stories.impact: Strong LIHTC availability and CRA/TIF leverage signal robust affordable housing capital in Miami-Dade, but limited signal for market-rate multifamily developers. However, the Naranja CRA/tif structure and density approval (115 units on affordable land) in an underserved south Miami-Dade submarket hint at potential for mixed-income or workforce housing development in the area as the metro tightens.

stories.title: Meal Delivery Service CookUnity to Open Flagship Storefront at NoMad Hotel
stories.excerpt: CookUnity, a subscription-based meal delivery platform, has inked 4,721 square feet of ground-floor retail space at Lam Group’s 1227 Broadway, also known as Virgin Hotel New York City, in Manhattan’s NoMad neighborhood, Commercial Observer has learned. The deal represents the chef-prepared meal service company’s first flagship storefront in New York City, a source close to the deal told CO. The store is expected to open in the first half of 2027. CookUnity launched in 2015 and is headquartered in Brooklyn at 630 Flushing Avenue. The company also has an existing Manhattan kitchen hub at 534 West 50th Street in Hell’s Kitchen and a Flatiron District office at 36 Lexington Avenue. It’s unclear whether its new NoMad spot represents a fourth location or a relocation of an existing site. The asking rent and the length of the lease were also not available. The average asking rent for retail space in the nearby neighborhood around Herald Square was $394 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to data from CBRE. The landlord was represented by Adam Weinblatt and Harley Bonn from Newmark, according to the source. CookUnity was represented by Alexandra Yanoff and Christine Nebiar from Brand Urban. Newmark declined to comment. “We’re excited to see CookUnity’s first flagship land at the Virgin Hotel in NoMad, a great signal for the brand’s evolution from delivery only to a space where people can finally interact with it in real life through fine dining and retail,” Nebiar said in a statement. CookUnity’s new flagship will feature dining and retail options, as well as space for community gatherings for lovers of the culinary arts. The company delivers in New York, Chicago, Los Angeles, Miami, Atlanta, Austin, Chicago, Seattle and Toronto. Virgin, a 38-story hotel tower on Broadway between West 29th and West 30th streets, opened in 2023 and provides guests with over 400 hotel rooms, a rooftop pool and restaurant Everdene, which o
bullets: CookUnity (meal delivery) leases 4,721 sq ft ground-floor retail at Virgin Hotel NoMad (1227 Broadway), Manhattan.
First flagship storefront in NYC; expected opening H1 2027.
Herald Square-area retail asking rent ~$394/sq ft (Q2 2026).
stories.summary: CookUnity, a Brooklyn-based meal delivery subscription service, signed a ground-floor retail lease at 1227 Broadway (Virgin Hotel New York), a Lam Group property in Manhattan's NoMad neighborhood. The 4,721-square-foot flagship storefront is expected to open in H1 2027. The deal marks CookUnity's first NYC flagship and expands its footprint (existing Manhattan locations in Hell's Kitchen and Flatiron).
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. Retail lease by a food-service startup in an existing hotel property does not signal demand, supply, capital, or cost shifts affecting apartment development. NoMad rents and submarket dynamics favor office/hospitality repositioning over new multifamily.

stories.title: Meet the Multifamily Power Players Defining the Future of Real Estate
stories.excerpt: Connect CRE’s fourth annual Leadership Series brings you an unfiltered perspective from the heavyweights driving investment, development, finance and operations. For the first time ever, these industry-shaping insights are available as an exclusive downloadable report and a digital profile series on connectcre.com. To put this series together, we invited the lenders, developers, operators and capital-markets voices who are defining where rental housing goes next.  With each bringing decades of experience to their senior-level positions, they’re uniquely qualified to assess the current state of multifamily as well as the near-term and long-term outlook.  We provided these 18 multifamily leaders with a range of questions, leaving it to their discretion to select the topics they wanted to discuss. Their responses are presented here in their own words. The choices of which questions to answer, as well as the responses themselves, underscore the priorities they see in the current market.  In alphabetical order, our 2026 Industry Leaders are as follows: Brenda Barrett, Apartment Management Consultants; Kelli Carhart, CBRE; Lili Dunn, Bell Partners, Inc.; Jim Flynn, Lument; Andrew Kadish, CAPREIT; Sharon Karaffa, Newmark; Garrett Karam, Embrey; Donald P. King III, Walker & Dunlop; Evan Kinne, GSP/AXCS Capital; Ray Lawler, Hines; Gregory MacDonald, Ballast; Nicholas Matus, BCE; Hessam Nadji, Marcus & Millichap; Ethan Penner, Hill Street Realty; Elie Rieder, Castle Lanterra; Ken Rogozinski, Greystone Housing Impact Investors L.P.; David Schwartz, Waterton; and Larry Taylor, Christina Development.  Each of the companies represented here is an industry leader along with the C-suite executives in the 2026 series. For
bullets: Connect CRE publishes 2026 Leadership Series: 18 multifamily industry heavyweights' unfiltered views.
Participants: developers, lenders, operators, capital-markets experts (Embrey, CBRE, Hines, etc.).
Topics chosen by leaders reflect current market priorities.
stories.summary: Connect CRE released its fourth annual Leadership Series featuring 18 multifamily industry leaders—lenders, developers, operators, and capital-markets experts—discussing priorities and outlook. Participants include executives from Embrey, CBRE, Hines, Lument, Marcus & Millichap, and others. The editorial approach lets leaders choose topics, making their selections a proxy for current market concerns.
stories.impact: No direct market signal. This is industry commentary and editorial content. Value lies in synthesis of leadership sentiment on market direction, but without specific quotes or data, cannot isolate actionable demand, supply, capital, or cost insights.

stories.title: Endeavor boosts Austin office pipeline with 5th and Walsh project
stories.excerpt: Endeavor’s 5th and Walsh project in Downtown Austin, which will also house its new headquarters, is finally getting off the ground.  Plans for the new five-story, 198,000-square-foot office mid-rise and retail building were submitted to the city in March 2022, more than four years ago. The development will feature 21,000 square feet of restaurant and retail space on the ground floor and a below-grade parking garage, according to the Austin Business Journal.  The project is expected to be completed in the later months of 2028, and Endeavor will promptly move into the building from its old headquarters at 500 West […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Endeavor's 5th & Walsh project (198k sq ft, 5-story office, retail) in downtown Austin targeting late 2028 completion.
50% preleased (85k sq ft locked + 25k Endeavor HQ = 110k of 198k); leasing ongoing.
Amazon exploring 45-year development agreement for 2,614-acre Endeavor-owned parcel (robotics manufacturing).
stories.summary: Endeavor is advancing its 5th & Walsh office project in downtown Austin—a 198,000-square-foot, five-story mid-rise with 21,000 sq ft of ground-floor retail and parking. Originally filed in March 2022, construction is expected to finish late 2028. Nearly 50% is preleased (85k sq ft + Endeavor's 25k-sq-ft HQ move). Separately, Amazon is in advanced talks to anchor Endeavor's 2,614-acre Dog's Head area (robotics manufacturing, 45-year agreement); City has approved annexation.
stories.impact: Austin office supply constraint and Amazon HQ/manufacturing presence could drive significant population and job inflow, lifting multifamily demand in Austin metro. However, this article is office-focused. The Amazon robotics facility is the real signal: major employer footprint outside downtown could pull residential demand toward suburban/central Texas submarkets. Developers should monitor Amazon deal closure and timeline for multifamily site acquisition/zoning in Dog's Head and surrounding submarkets.

stories.title: It’s Finally Showtime Again at Hollywood’s Cinerama Dome
stories.excerpt: Get your popcorn ready, Los Angeles. One of Hollywood’s most beloved movie theaters is finally coming back. Sony Pictures Entertainment and its Alamo Drafthouse Cinema subsidiary announced Wednesday they struck a long-term lease to restore and reopen the Cinerama Dome on Sunset Boulevard and the adjacent 14-screen former ArcLight Cinemas complex, six years after both went dark during the pandemic. “Hot damn! We’re bringing the Dome back, baby,” Tom Rothman, chairman and CEO of Sony’s Motion Picture Group, said in a statement. “We believe in moviegoing down to our soul, and no venue on Earth stands more for that than the one-of-a-kind Dome. I am deeply proud that Sony is investing in Hollywood, literally.” The Cinerama Dome is expected to reopen in early 2028, with renovations beginning next month to preserve its 86-foot-wide curved screen and large-format presentation. JLL’s Ken Shishido represented Alamo Drafthouse in the lease. CBRE’s Nicole Mihalka, Muhlstein Commercial’s Carl Muhlstein and JLL’s Scott Burns and Shauna Mattis represented the ownership of the Dome Entertainment Center. Alamo will operate the landmark, but the Dome will not offer dine-in service like at typical Alamo theaters. The neighboring former ArcLight multiplex will reopen as an Alamo Drafthouse with dine-in service, 4K laser projection and 70-millimeter cinematic capabilities. Sony acquired Alamo Drafthouse in 2024. The deal carries far more significance in addition to filling a long-vacant theater complex. “This lease marks the beginning of an exciting new chapter for one of Hollywood’s most iconic and beloved properties,” Mihalka said in a statement. “The Cinerama Dome has been a cultural landmark for generations, and bringing Sony Entertainment and Alamo Drafthouse to the Dome Entertainment Center is a strong vote of confidence in the future of theatrical entertainment and the continued demand for shared moviegoing experiences.” The unmistakable landmark opened in 1963. It’s a Los
bullets: Sony Pictures & Alamo Drafthouse announced long-term lease for Cinerama Dome & 14-screen ArcLight complex.
Reopening early 2028 after pandemic closure; renovations begin next month.
Sony CEO: 'we believe in moviegoing down to our soul' and investing in Hollywood.
stories.summary: Sony Pictures Entertainment and Alamo Drafthouse Cinema announced a long-term lease to restore and reopen the iconic Cinerama Dome and adjacent 14-screen ArcLight Cinemas complex on Hollywood's Sunset Boulevard. Both venues have been closed since the pandemic. Renovations start next month with reopening targeted for early 2028. Sony's Motion Picture Group CEO framed the investment as a demonstration of faith in theatrical moviegoing and Hollywood real estate.
stories.impact: Same as Article 1—limited direct multifamily relevance. Entertainment anchor reopening in Hollywood supports mixed-use vitality and foot traffic, but LA Hollywood submarket is supply-constrained with high barriers to new development; unlikely to spur new apartment construction.

stories.title: Bascom Buys 183-Unit Orange County, Calif., Complex for $53M
stories.excerpt: The Bascom Group has acquired a 183-unit apartment complex in Orange County, Calif., as the company expands its multifamily holdings this year in the Western U.S. The Irvine, Calif.-based investment firm put down $53.1 million for Castlewood Park Apartments in Buena Park. The deal is equal to about $290,200 per unit. Institutional Property Advisors (IPA), a division of Marcus & Millichap, announced the deal after the brokerage’s Brian Eisendrath, Cameron Chalfant, Jesse Zarouk and Jake Vitta arranged the financing. IPA’s Kevin Green, Joe Grabiec and Gregory Harris represented the seller, which was not disclosed. Property records show Newcastle owned the property for more than 30 years. IPA described Buena Park as one of the most supply-constrained submarkets in Orange County. “Castlewood Park is the largest multifamily sale to transact in Buena Park and one of only eight $50 million-plus multifamily sales in north Orange County in the last 24 months,” Green said in a statement. Built in 1963, Castlewood Park includes 46 buildings across 8.7 acres at 6968 Campus Drive, adjacent to Buena Park High School. The community features of two-, three- and four-bedroom apartments averaging 1,028 square feet. It was renovated a few times from 2010 through 2023. Bascom plans to renovate apartment interiors, upgrade the property’s amenities, and add full-time on-site management. Bascom said it has acquired 13 apartment communities totaling 3,231 units for more than $930 million since federal interest rates started rising after the pandemic. Earlier this year, Bascom acquired a 294-unit property in Las Vegas and a 289-unit in Tucson, Ariz. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Bascom Group acquires 183-unit Castlewood Park (Buena Park, Orange County) for $53.1M ($290.2k/unit).
Built 1963, 46 buildings, 8.7 acres; largest MF sale in Buena Park, one of only eight $50M+ sales in north OC in 24 months.
Buena Park described as 'most supply-constrained' submarket in Orange County.
stories.summary: Bascom Group acquired Castlewood Park Apartments, a 183-unit complex in Buena Park, Orange County, for $53.1 million ($290,200/unit). The 1963-built property spans 46 buildings across 8.7 acres. IPA (Marcus & Millichap division) noted this is the largest multifamily transaction in Buena Park and just one of eight $50 million-plus multifamily sales in north Orange County over the past 24 months. Buena Park is described as one of the most supply-constrained submarkets in Orange County.
stories.impact: Supply constraint in Buena Park/north Orange County signals rental demand resilience but limited new-build opportunity. High per-unit price ($290k) reflects scarcity; developers should explore infill/redevelopment opportunities in supply-tight north OC submarkets or scout adjacent areas with more entitlement/land availability to capture absorbed demand.

stories.title: Starwood files data center plan as Virginia residents revolt
stories.excerpt: Barry Sternlicht’s Northern Virginia data center drive is forging ahead, even as residents plan to revolt against Starwood Capital Group’s dreams. Starwood filed a permit application for its two-building project at the 34-acre Plaza 500 on the Alexandria town line in Virginia, the Washington Business Journal reported. The first building, Plaza 500 South, is planned for more than 226,000 square feet, according to the pending application. HKS Inc. is the architect for the project. The first phase of construction is expected to carry a $5 million price tag, according to Fairfax County records. In addition to the two data center […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Starwood Capital files permit for two-building data center complex at Plaza 500, Alexandria/Fairfax County border, Virginia.
First phase (Plaza 500 South): 226k sq ft, $5M initial cost; substation within 100 feet of residential.
Resident opposition; Starwood also developing Herndon data center campus; purchased site for $165M four years ago.
stories.summary: Starwood Capital Group filed a permit application for a two-building data center project at Plaza 500 (34-acre site on Alexandria/Fairfax County border). The first phase, Plaza 500 South, is planned for over 226,000 square feet with an estimated $5 million initial construction cost. Starwood also proposes an on-site substation within 100 feet of homes in the Bren Mar neighborhood, sparking resident opposition. Starwood purchased the site four years ago for $165 million and is simultaneously developing a data center campus in nearby Herndon.
stories.impact: Data center expansion in Northern Virginia (Alexandria/Fairfax) signals strong tech/cloud infrastructure demand, but does NOT directly affect multifamily supply, demand, or development. However, proximity to Amazon HQ2 (Crystal City) and ongoing tech/defense contractor growth in DC region suggests sustained job/population inflow into Northern Virginia, supporting long-term apartment demand across Fairfax, Arlington, and Alexandria submarkets. Monitor Starwood's timeline and ancillary commercial/residential mixed-use opportunities near data center corridors.

stories.title: JP Morgan Recaps Industrial Portfolio With $209M Loan
stories.excerpt: A joint venture between Centerbridge Partners and Henderson Group has landed a $208.5 million debt package to recapitalize 40 industrial assets in Pennsylvania and South Florida, Commercial Observer has learned. J.P. Morgan Chase supplied the five-year, floating-rate loan on the sponsorship’s logistics portfolio comprising 2.3 million square feet and featuring a mix of last-mile warehouses, distribution sites and shallow-bay facilities. The properties, which have buildings ranging from 16,000 to 155,000 square feet, are 95 percent leased to 110 tenants. CBRE negotiated the debt with a team consisting of Tom Traynor, Tom Rugg, Mark Finan and Henry Fenmore. J.P. Morgan, Centerbridge and Henderson Group did not immediately return requests for comment. CBRE declined to comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: JP Morgan provides $208.5M floating-rate loan to recapitalize 40 industrial assets (2.3M sq ft) in Pennsylvania & South Florida.
Portfolio: 95% leased to 110 tenants; mix of last-mile, distribution, shallow-bay facilities.
Joint venture of Centerbridge Partners & Henderson Group; five-year term.
stories.summary: Centerbridge Partners and Henderson Group obtained a $208.5 million five-year floating-rate debt package from JP Morgan to recapitalize a 40-property industrial portfolio comprising 2.3 million square feet across Pennsylvania and South Florida. The portfolio is 95% leased to 110 tenants and includes last-mile warehouses, distribution centers, and shallow-bay facilities. CBRE negotiated the transaction.
stories.impact: No direct multifamily relevance. Industrial portfolio refinancing and capital availability signal broader credit market liquidity, but does not affect apartment supply, demand, or development feasibility. South Florida mention is incidental; no signal regarding residential markets.

stories.title: Vlad Doronin joining forces with South Korea juggernaut
stories.excerpt: Vlad Doronin’s global conquest for the Aman brand is taking the company to South Korea and beyond. Doronin’s OKO Group and the Shinsegae Group, one of the top retail conglomerates in the Asian nation, formed a fund to develop luxury hotels and more, Bloomberg reported. The fund is being launched with an initial $500 million investment. The partners are kicking things off with a mixed-use project in Seoul’s Gangnam District, which will include an Aman hotel, residences and a club, according to Doronin. As they continue to fundraise, the partners will expand out into more of Asia, the Middle East […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Vlad Doronin's OKO Group & Shinsegae Group (South Korea retail conglomerate) form $500M luxury development fund.
Initial project: mixed-use Seoul Gangnam (Aman hotel, residences, club); expand to Asia, Middle East, US.
Aman sold $9B branded residences in 4 years; ~20 hotels under construction; Beverly Hills penthouse targeted at $200M.
stories.summary: Vlad Doronin's OKO Group partnered with Shinsegae Group, a top South Korean retail conglomerate, to launch a $500 million fund for luxury hotel and residential developments. The inaugural project is a mixed-use complex in Seoul's Gangnam District featuring an Aman hotel, residences, and club. The fund will expand across Asia, the Middle East, and the United States. Aman has sold $9 billion in branded residences over four years and boasts approximately 20 hotels under construction globally.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to mainstream US multifamily development. Aman's high-end branded residences (luxury condos, not rentals) and international focus serve a niche ultra-luxury market. US impact limited to Beverly Hills and NYC; unlikely to affect apartment supply/demand in core rental markets. Monitor for any ancillary workforce housing or mixed-income components in US projects, but primary thesis is ultra-luxury condo sales, not institutional multifamily.

stories.title: Grubb Properties forms $1.9B apartment REIT after recapitalization
stories.excerpt: Grubb Properties wants a bigger balance sheet and bigger financing, forming a multifamily real estate investment trust to that end. The Charlotte-based developer formed a REIT by consolidating several of its funds, Bisnow reported. Link Apartments REIT, which will not be traded or opened up for additional investment, counts 45 properties and more than 5,600 units as part of its portfolio, valued at approximately $1.9 billion. Perhaps the most notable property in the portfolio is 8 Carlisle, a rental tower under construction in Manhattan’s Financial District. This month, Grubb secured a $377 million construction loan for its 462-unit rental project. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Grubb Properties forms Link Apartments REIT (nontraded): 45 properties, 5,600+ units, ~$1.9B portfolio valuation.
Notable: 8 Carlisle (Manhattan Financial District rental tower, 462 units); secured $377M construction loan this month.
Total financing across transactions: $617M (includes $240M credit facility for REIT + OZ REIT).
stories.summary: Grubb Properties consolidated multiple funds into Link Apartments REIT, a nontraded trust comprising 45 properties and 5,600+ units valued at approximately $1.9 billion. The portfolio includes 8 Carlisle, a 462-unit, 64-story rental tower under construction in Manhattan's Financial District, which just closed a $377 million construction loan (combining $300M from Maxim Capital and $77M mezzanine from Skylight). Grubb also secured a $240 million credit facility for both the REIT and a separate Link Apartments Opportunity Zone REIT. Total financing across all transactions reached $617 million.
stories.impact: Strong capital access and REIT formation signal investor appetite for stabilized multifamily portfolios and construction financing availability. Grubb's $617M financing haul and 8 Carlisle's $377M construction loan suggest lenders remain active in prime Manhattan rental markets despite rising rates. Developers should note: large-scale portfolios and REIT vehicles unlock bigger financing; concentrated Manhattan development (789-ft, 8th-tallest residential tower) demonstrates market hunger for trophy urban rental. Capital remains accessible for institutional-grade projects.

stories.title: CDK Lending Supplies $37M Loan for Newark Apartments Project
stories.excerpt: RBH Group has landed $36.7 million of pre-development financing for a mixed-use multifamily project in Newark, N.J., Commercial Observer has learned. CDK Lending provided the loan on the developer’s planned 420-unit apartment complex with ground-floor retail space at 101 Market Street in Newark’s Four Corners neighborhood near downtown. The debt pays off a previous loan provided by Goldman Sachs for the long-planned development, which is located within a 5.65-acre land assemblage site. Meridian Capital Group arranged the transaction with a team led by David Bollag and Naphtali Marrus. “Newark’s downtown has seen remarkable momentum over the past decade, and a development-ready assemblage of this scale in the central business district is exceptionally hard to come by,” Bollag said in a statement. RBH Group has been assembling land for a mixed-use development consisting of 49 separate contiguous lots since 2005, according to Bollag. The firm also plans to commence a 241-unit multifamily project at nearby 193 Market Street next year and is eyeing other developments at the site roughly half a mile from Newark Penn Station. Newark-based RBH Group is also developing the Teachers Village project at 243 Halsey Street in Newark featuring 203 apartments geared toward local educators. CDK Lending and RBH Group did not immediately return requests for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: CDK Lending provides $36.7M pre-development financing for RBH Group's 420-unit mixed-use project, Newark NJ (Four Corners).
Site: 101 Market Street, 5.65-acre land assemblage (49 separate lots, assembled since 2005).
Repays prior Goldman Sachs loan; Meridian Capital arranged; downtown Newark momentum cited.
stories.summary: CDK Lending provided $36.7 million in pre-development financing for RBH Group's 420-unit mixed-use apartment complex with ground-floor retail at 101 Market Street in Newark's Four Corners neighborhood. The project sits on a 5.65-acre land assemblage RBH has been piecing together since 2005 (49 contiguous lots). The debt replaces a previous Goldman Sachs loan. Meridian Capital arranged the transaction, citing downtown Newark's momentum over the past decade and the rarity of development-ready assemblages of this scale in the CBD.
stories.impact: Newark downtown multifamily development pipeline remains active and financed. Land assemblage complexity (49 lots, 20-year acquisition period) highlights regulatory/title friction but eventual financing approval signals lender confidence in Newark's CBDrevitalization and apartment demand. Developers should monitor Newark Four Corners/downtown for absorption and rental rate trends; pre-development financing suggests RBH expects market traction within 2–3 years. Capital availability in secondary Northeast CBDs remains strong for institutional sponsors.

stories.title: NYC’s top construction permits: Week ending July 17, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 New Building: The largest new building application filed last week was on the border of Crown Heights and Prospect-Lefferts Gardens. Rafael Rabinowitz of HSD Construction plans to build a 112,279-square-foot, eight-story development at 103 Empire Boulevard. The mixed-use project is slated to have 50 apartments. Kao Hwa Lee is the architect. Job Filing Number: B01408304-I1 📊 New Building: Borough Developers filed plans to construct an 82,731-square-foot mixed-use project at 45-51 and 45-49 Vernon Boulevard in Queens. The building will stand 10 stories tall and have 86 apartments. Kao Hwa Lee is the architect. Job Filing Numbers: Q01425664-I1, Q01425663-I1 📊 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: NYC permits (week ending July 17, 2026): 112k sq ft mixed-use (50 apts, Crown Heights/Prospect-Lefferts); 82.7k sq ft (86 apts, Queens Vernon).
Bronx: two 9-story buildings, 99 units each (128.7k sq ft, Tremont); 192k sq ft alteration (Brookfield Properties, Financial District).
Steady multifamily pipeline across all boroughs; no major density or zoning shifts noted.
stories.summary: NYC construction permits filed week ending July 17, 2026 included: (1) 112,279-sq-ft, 8-story mixed-use with 50 apartments in Crown Heights/Prospect-Lefferts; (2) 82,731-sq-ft, 10-story mixed-use with 86 apartments in Queens (Vernon Boulevard); (3) two 9-story Bronx apartment buildings with 99 units each (128,738 sq ft, Tremont); (4) 192,245-sq-ft alteration at 250 Vestry Street (Brookfield, Financial District). Approvals span residential, mixed-use, commercial alteration.
stories.impact: NYC multifamily pipeline remains active across all boroughs (Manhattan, Queens, Bronx) with steady small-to-medium projects (50–99 units). No signal of supply surge or regulatory constraint relief; density remains modest per permit. Entitlement velocity appears normal. Developers should track Queens and Bronx permits for submarket opportunities; Manhattan Financial District retail/office conversion hints at emerging residential interest in secondary locations (250 Vestry = office-to-residential pipeline).
